extra.reasoning:
The property is monotonicity in dizziness: flipping dizziness false->true (all else fixed) must never lower acuity. Enum order low->high is [self_care, routine, urgent, emergent].

dizziness appears in exactly one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100, the highest), which fires only when all 12 of {leg_swelling, slurred_speech, sweating, dyspnea, confusion, pallor, fever, chest_pain, focal_weakness, back_pain, rash, dizziness} are true and sets acuity := self_care (the LOWEST acuity). dizziness appears in no other rule.

Counterexample: set the other 11 conjuncts true (leg_swelling, slurred_speech, sweating, dyspnea, confusion, pallor, fever, chest_pain, focal_weakness, back_pain, rash = true), severe_headache = false (irrelevant).

Before (dizziness=false): interaction_drop does NOT fire (needs dizziness). Many priority-3 rules match (raise_pallor, raise_slurred_speech, raise_chest_pain, raise_back_pain, raise_dyspnea -> emergent); priority-2 rules also match. Highest matching priority = 3, so acuity = emergent (highest).

After (dizziness=true): all 12 conjuncts now true, so interaction_drop fires. Its priority 100 beats all priority-3 rules, so acuity := self_care (lowest).

Result: emergent -> self_care, a decrease in acuity caused solely by setting dizziness true. This violates monotonicity. The witness_assignment is the post-flip (dizziness=true) input that yields the lowered acuity self_care, whereas the same input with dizziness=false yields emergent.
